questions.question: What is the first step to make a Notion page visible as a website?

options.text: Enable "Share to web" for the page
options.explanation: Turning on "Share to web" makes the page publicly accessible, which is required before it can be used as a website.

options.text: Add a custom domain in Notion settings
options.explanation: Custom domains are added after the page is already shared publicly; they are not the first step.

options.text: Create a new workspace for the site
options.explanation: A separate workspace is unnecessary; any page in an existing workspace can be published.

options.text: Install a third‑party plugin for publishing
options.explanation: Notion’s built‑in "Share to web" feature does not require external plugins.

questions.question: Which of the following options can you customize directly in Notion’s website publishing settings?

options.text: Site favicon
options.explanation: Notion allows you to upload a favicon that appears in browser tabs for the published site.

options.text: Meta description for SEO
options.explanation: Notion does not currently provide a field to edit meta descriptions; you’d need external tools for SEO.

options.text: Custom CSS styles
options.explanation: Notion does not support custom CSS in its native publishing workflow.

options.text: Server‑side redirects
options.explanation: Redirects must be handled outside of Notion, typically via the domain provider.

questions.question: If you want to use your own domain (e.g., www.mybrand.com) with a Notion‑published site, which service must you configure?

options.text: Notion’s internal DNS server
options.explanation: Notion does not provide its own DNS service; you must use an external DNS provider.

options.text: Your domain registrar’s DNS records
options.explanation: You point a CNAME (or ALIAS) record from your registrar to Notion’s publishing domain to link the custom domain.

options.text: A third‑party static site generator
options.explanation: Static site generators are unrelated to connecting a custom domain to Notion’s live page.

options.text: A VPN tunnel to Notion’s servers
options.explanation: VPNs are not used for domain mapping; DNS configuration is the correct method.

questions.question: Why might a Notion‑published site have limited SEO performance compared to a traditional website?

options.text: Notion pages cannot be indexed by search engines
options.explanation: Search engines can index Notion pages; the limitation is not total exclusion.

options.text: Notion does not let you add structured data, meta tags, or custom titles for each page
options.explanation: Without control over meta tags and structured data, SEO optimization is constrained.

options.text: Notion forces all pages to load over HTTP, not HTTPS
options.explanation: Notion serves pages over HTTPS, so this is not the issue.

options.text: Notion limits the number of pages you can publish
options.explanation: Page count does not directly affect SEO; the key factor is lack of SEO‑specific controls.

questions.question: Which of the following statements about Notion’s "Share to web" link is true?

options.text: The link can be password‑protected through Notion settings
options.explanation: Notion does not offer native password protection for public links.

options.text: Anyone with the link can view the page, even without a Notion account
options.explanation: The public link grants view access to anyone, regardless of account status.

options.text: The link automatically expires after 30 days
options.explanation: Public links remain active until you manually disable "Share to web".

options.text: The link can be edited to change the page’s URL slug
options.explanation: The URL slug is generated by Notion and cannot be altered via the link.

questions.question: When publishing a multi‑page Notion site, how can you ensure visitors can navigate between pages without exposing the internal Notion hierarchy?

options.text: Create a master page with linked sub‑pages and enable "Full‑width" view
options.explanation: While linking helps navigation, "Full‑width" view does not hide the hierarchy; visitors still see the Notion UI.

options.text: Use Notion’s "Table of contents" block on each page and hide the sidebar via the "Hide navigation" toggle
options.explanation: The Table of Contents provides internal navigation, and the "Hide navigation" toggle removes the Notion sidebar, giving a clean website feel.

options.text: Export each page as HTML and host them on an external server
options.explanation: Exporting defeats the purpose of using Notion’s live publishing feature.

options.text: Add a third‑party JavaScript navigation bar through an embed block
options.explanation: Notion does not allow custom JavaScript, so this approach is not possible.

questions.question: What limitation should you consider when deciding whether Notion is the right tool for a high‑traffic commercial website?

options.text: Notion caps the number of concurrent visitors to 100 per page
options.explanation: There is no explicit hard cap, but performance can degrade under heavy load.

options.text: Notion’s publishing infrastructure is not optimized for large‑scale traffic and lacks advanced performance controls like CDN caching rules
options.explanation: While Notion provides basic CDN caching, it does not give fine‑grained control needed for high‑traffic sites.

options.text: Notion does not support HTTPS, making it unsuitable for secure transactions
options.explanation: Notion serves pages over HTTPS by default.

options.text: Notion requires a paid plan for any public website, increasing cost for commercial use
options.explanation: Public publishing is available on free plans; cost is not the primary limitation for traffic.
